coredata.dc:title: An aptamer biosensor for CA125 quantification in human serum based on upconversion luminescence resonance energy transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaojing
Wang, Yanying
Deng, Hongping
Xiong, Xiaoxing
Zhang, Huijuan
Liang, Tao
Li, Chunya
coredata.dc:description: CA125 is the most widely used biomarker for the early detection of ovarian cancer. Luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET)-based probes show pronounced specificity and simplicity for CA125 detection, but they suffer from strong interference from autofluorescence of biosamples. To resolve this problem, near-infrared (NIR)-excitable upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) was employed as energy donor to fabricate CA125 biosensor. Carbon dots (CDs) were utilized as energy acceptor for the overlap between the upconversion luminescence (UCL) spectrum of UCNPs and the absorption spectrum of CDs. Aptamer-modified UCNPs were combined with CDs through π-π stacking interaction, which triggered the LRET process and induce UCL quenching with a high quenching efficiency of up to 90%. In presence of CA125, the formation of CA125-aptamer complex blocked the π-π stacking and recovered UCL. The UCL intensity increased linearly with the logarithm of CA125 concentration in the range from 0.01 to 100 U·mL−1. A detection limit of 9.0 × 10−3 U·mL−1 was obtained. This probe is also available for CA125 detection in human serum and may be a useful tool for the early detection of ovarian cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CA125
Ovarian cancer
Upconversion nanoparticles
Luminescence resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CA125 detection in aqueous solution and human serum

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from The National Natural Science Foundation of China , China (Nos. 21675175 , 21874157 and 21275166 ), Major Projects of Technical Innovation of Hubei Province , China (No. 2017ACA172 ) and The Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province , China (Nos. 2018CFB617 and 2015CFA092 ).

The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from The National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (Nos. 21675175, 21874157 and 21275166), Major Projects of Technical Innovation of Hubei Province, China (No. 2017ACA172) and The Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province, China (Nos. 2018CFB617 and 2015CFA092).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopic images (a), histograms of the size distribution (b) and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) images (c) of OA-UCNPs; (d) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of OA-UCNPs, bare UCNPs, PAA and PAA-UCNPs.

(a) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of the as-synthesized CDs; (b) TEM image of the as-synthesized CDs; (c) the size distribution for the CDs; (d) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CDs and UCL spectrum of PAA-UCNPs (in water).

UCL spectra (a) and UCL intensity at 544 nm (b) of Apt-UCNPs in presence of various concentrations of CDs. F and F0 represent UCL intensity of Apt-UCNPs in absence and presence of CDs, respectively. F and F0 represent UCL intensity of Apt-UCNPs in absence and presence of CDs, respectively.
(a) UCL spectra of UCNPs-CDs with various concentrations of CA125 in HEPES buffer; (b) The relationship between the UCL intensity at 544 nm with the logarithm of CA125 concentration.
(a) UCL spectra of UCNPs-CDs with various concentrations of CA125 in 100-fold diluted human serum; (b) The relationship between the UCL intensity at 544 nm with the logarithm of CA125 concentration.
Schematic illustration of the construction of the nanoprobe and sensing of CA125 through LRET.
Analytical results of CA125 assay in 100-fold diluted human serum samples from patients with ovarian cancer using the nanoprobe CDs-UCNPs.

head.ce:title: An aptamer biosensor for CA125 quantification in human serum based on upconversion luminescence resonance energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry of the State Ethnic Affairs Commission, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry of the State Ethnic Affairs Commission
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry of the State Ethnic Affairs Commission, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Vascular Surgery and Central Laboratory, Renmin Hospital of Wuhan University, Wuhan 430060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Vascular Surgery and Central Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Vascular Surgery and Central Laboratory, Renmin Hospital of Wuhan University, Wuhan 430060, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CA125 is the most widely used biomarker for the early detection of ovarian cancer. Luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET)-based probes show pronounced specificity and simplicity for CA125 detection, but they suffer from strong interference from autofluorescence of biosamples. To resolve this problem, near-infrared (NIR)-excitable upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) was employed as energy donor to fabricate CA125 biosensor. Carbon dots (CDs) were utilized as energy acceptor for the overlap between the upconversion luminescence (UCL) spectrum of UCNPs and the absorption spectrum of CDs. Aptamer-modified UCNPs were combined with CDs through π-π stacking interaction, which triggered the LRET process and induce UCL quenching with a high quenching efficiency of up to 90%. In presence of CA125, the formation of CA125-aptamer complex blocked the π-π stacking and recovered UCL. The UCL intensity increased linearly with the logarithm of CA125 concentration in the range from 0.01 to 100 U·mL−1. A detection limit of 9.0 × 10−3 U·mL−1 was obtained. This probe is also available for CA125 detection in human serum and may be a useful tool for the early detection of ovarian cancer.

ce:section.ce:para: Ovarian cancer is one of the most fatal malignancies threatening the lives of women. The survival rate closely relates to the stage of the cancer at the time of diagnosis, which can be significantly improved by early diagnosis [1–3]. Since the concentrations of some specific biomolecules, i.e. biomarkers, increase in serum before obvious tissue morphological change during the development of cancer, biomarker recognition methods show great advantage in early diagnosis of cancer. Carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA125), firstly discovered by Bast et al. in 1981 [4], is the most widely used ovarian cancer biomarker approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). The concentration of CA125 is generally lower than 35 U·mL−1 in healthy women but elevated in 90% of women with ovarian cancer [5,6]. Therefore, it is urgently to develop reliable probes for CA125 detection to achieve early diagnose of ovarian cancer.

Various detection methods have been exploited for biological and clinical analysis of CA125, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [7,8], capillary electrophoretic enzyme immunoassay [9], electrochemical immunoassay [10], microfluidic chip electrophoresis [11], photoelectric immunoassay [12]. However, these methods suffer from complicated experimental procedures or low sensitivity [12]. Luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET) is a homogeneous assay technique based on the nonradiative energy transfer from energy donors to energy acceptors via dipole–dipole interactions within close proximity (normally < 10 nm) [13]. Through changing the distance or spectral overlap between energy donor and energy acceptor to modulate LRET process, the luminescence of energy accepter can be quenched or recovered in the presence of targets, which can be used to construct “turn off”-type or “turn on”-type fluorescent probes. Specially, “turn-on”-type luminescent probes show higher signal-to-background (SBR) than “turn-off”-type ones [14], which have been used in CA125 detection [15–17]. However, these probes are excited by visible light, which introduce strong autofluorescence from biosamples and bring interference to detection results.

Lanthanide ions-doped upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) can subsequently absorb two or more near-infrared (NIR) photons and then emit a UV–vis-NIR photon with higher energy [18–23]. Because biomolecules can be hardly excited by NIR photons, autofluorescence from biosamples can be effectively eliminated. Besides, UCNPs feature large anti-Stokes shift [24], sharp and multiple emissions, and high photostability [25], which make them pretty suitable for constructing LRET-based luminescent probes [26–31]. Herein, we constructed a “turn on”-type CA125 biosensor based on upconversion luminescence resonance energy transfer, in which UCNPs was served as energy acceptor. CA125 aptamer was modified on the surface of UCNPs, and then combined with carbon dots (CDs), the energy acceptor, through π-π stacking interaction. Because of the enough proximity of UCNPs and CDs, LRET process occurred and induced the quenching of upconversion luminescence (UCL). In presence of CA125, the formation of CA125-aptamer complex blocked the π-π stacking interaction and inhibited LRET process, resulting in the recovery of UCL. The probe shows great sensitivity to CA125 with a detection limit of 9 × 10−3 U·mL−1. This probe could be used to detect the concentration of CA125 both in HEPES buffer and human serum. The results indicate CA125 concentration in clinical serum samples are all higher than critical value for health person. With excellent sensitivity and selectivity, good thermodynamic stability and pH stability, this probe may provide a powerful method for early diagnose of ovarian cancer.

All chemicals were of analytical grade or better and were used as received without further purification. 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl-aminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl) were purchased from TCI Chemicals (Shanghai, China). 1-Octadecene (ODE), Oleic acid (OA), dopamine hydrochloride, 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES) and 4-(2-Hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polyacrylic acid (with an average molecular weight of 1800), was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Amine modified CA125 aptamer (5′-AAAAAACTCACTATAGGGAGACAAGAATAAACGCTC AA-3′) was supplied by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other chemical reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired by D8 ADVANCE (BRUKER, Germany). The crystal phase of UCNPs were identified by a Bruker D8 Discover X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with 2θ range from 10° to 70° at a scanning rate of 4° per minute, with Cu Ka irradiation (k = 1.5406 Å). The sizes and morphologies of polyacrylic acid (PAA) modified NaYF4:Yb, Er UCNPs and CDs were characterized by FEI Tecnai G2 20S-TWIN transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images (FEI Company, Netherlands). FT-IR spectra of PAA-UCNPs were measured on Magan-IR Spectrometer 500 (Nicolet, Madision, WI, USA) with the KBr pellet technique. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of carbon nanoparticles was acquired using a UV-2550 UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). A 980 nm diode CW laser (Beijing Hi-Tech Optoelectronic Co., Ltd.) was used as the excitation source with the power being set at 500 mW. The upconverting fluorescence spectra were recorded on DCS200PC Photon Counting with single-photon sensitivity through an Omni-λ 300 monochromator (Beijing Zolix Instruments Co., Ltd.).

Ln2O3 (Y3+:Yb3+:Er3+ = 78:20:2) and excess hydrochloric acid were added to a single-necked flask. The mixture was heated to 60 °C for 1 h to dissolve Ln2O3. The obtained LnCl3 was cooled down to room temperature and added ultrapure water, ethanol, hexane. The mixture was stirred for 5 h at 70 °C. The water phased was removed and the organic phase was washed with a mixture of water and EtOH (v/v = 1:1) for three times. The solvent was removed by reduced pressure distillation and the obtained Ln(oleate)3 were finally dissolved in a mixture of oleic acid (OA) and 1-octadecene (ODE) for further use.

NaYF4:Yb,Er UCNPs were prepared by a solvothermal method. 1 mmol Ln(oleate)3 (Y3+:Yb3+:Er3+ = 78:20:2), 20 mmol NaF, 6 mL OA and 6 mL ODE were added to a three-necked flask. The mixture was heated to 50 °C for 1 h under an argon flow. The temperature was raised to 290 °C and kept for 2 h and then cooled down to room temperature. An equal volume of EtOH was added to precipitate the nanoparticles and then washed by hexane/EtOH (1:1). Finally, the synthesized OA-UCNPs was dispersed in chloroform.

100 mg OA-UCNPs were added with 50 mL ethanol and 1 mL concentrated hydrochloric acid and then sonicated for 1 h to remove OA ligands. The bare UCNPs were collected by centrifugation and washed with ethanol and water subsequently. The bare UCNPs (10 mg) were added to 10 mL ultrapure water containing 50 nmol PAA. The mixture was shaken for 12 h at room temperature. PAA-UCNPs were collected by centrifugation and washed with ultrapure water. Finally, PAA-UCNPs were dispersed in ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C.

O-phenylenediamine (1.0 g) and dopamine (0.5 g) were dissolved in 50 mL water with hydrochloric acid solution (pH = 1) and then transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL). The solution was heated to 180 °C and kept for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was neutralized by NaOH (0.1 M). The products were collected by centrifugation and washed with water for three times. Finally, CDs were dispersed in ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C.

Amino modified CA125 aptamer was covalently conjugated to PAA-UCNPs using EDC·HCl and Sulfo-NHS as the cross-linking agents. Briefly, 100 μL PAA-UCNPs (10 mg·mL−1) were added to 700 μL of MES buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 5.5). Then EDC·HCl (0.2 mg) and Sulfo-NHS (0.6 mg) were added and the mixture solution was incubated with gentle shaking at 37 °C for 1 h to activate the carboxyl groups of PAA-UCNPs. The activated PAA-UCNPs were collected by centrifugation. The obtained precipitates were dispersed in 1 mL of HEPES buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.4) and then added with 1 nmol CA125 aptamer. The mixture was incubated 12 h at 37 °C and then 10 mg Tris-HCl was added to the mixture to block the excess NHS. Subsequently, the CA125 aptamer-modified UCNPs (Apt-UCNPs) were harvested by centrifugation and washed with ultrapure water. Finally, Apt-UCNPs were dispersed in 1 mL of HEPES buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.4) and stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CA125 detection in aqueous solution and human serum
ce:section.ce:para: For a typical LRET assay in aqueous buffer, CDs (0.1 mg) were added to Apt-UCNPs (1 mg·mL−1) in HEPES (10 mM, pH = 7.4) or 100-fold diluted human serum (with 10 mM HEPES buffer, pH = 7.4. The mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 80 min. Then various amounts of CA125 were added and incubated for another 100 min. UCL was excited with 980 nm continuous-wave laser and the data in the range of 470 nm to 600 nm was collected. Each sample was measured independently for three times.

As shown in Scheme 1 , the CA125 probe UCNPs-CDs was constructed based on LRET from UCNPs to CDs. Spectral overlap and short distance (generally < 10 nm) are two prerequisites of LRET, thus CDs were chosen as energy acceptor. CDs show strong absorption in the range of 400–700 nm, which overlaps with the green UCL of UCNPs, and the π-π stacking interaction between CDs and the aptamer of Apt-UCNPs can shorten the distance between UCNPs and CDs. The LRET process from UCNPs to CDs induce the quenching of UCL. In presence of CA125, the formation of CA125-aptamer complex blocked the interaction between CDs with the aptamer and prohibited the LRET process. As a sequence, UCL intensity increased in a CA125-dependent manner, which allows the quantitative detection of CA125.

NaYF4: Yb, Er were employed to construct UCL probe, in which energy transfer occurs through this energy-transfer cascade: Yb3+ → Er3+ → CDs. UCNPs were firstly synthetized with OA as coating agent. The TEM image illustrate that the synthetized OA-UCNPs have spherical morphology and a uniform size with an average diameter of 41.7 nm ± 3.6 nm (Fig. 1 a, b). XRD patterns show OA-UCNPs were hexagonal phase, which were consistent with standard hexagonal NaYF4 lattice (JCPDS No. 16-0334) (Fig. 1c). It is known that UCNPs with hexagonal phase show higher UCL efficiency than those with cubic phase, which confers the great detection performance of the constructed CA125 probe. To connect CA125 aptamer with UCNPs, OA ligands on the surface of OA-UCNPs was removed and then substituted by polyacrylic acid (PAA). FTIR spectra clearly shows the removal of OA and the introduction of PAA (Fig. 1d), for the disappearance of the band around 1709 cm−1 band (CO stretching vibration of OA) in bare UCNPs and the appearance of the band around 1709 cm−1 band (CO stretching vibration of PAA) and 1565 cm−1 band (COO− symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching vibration of PAA). The amine functionalized aptamers were then accomplished by interacting with PAA-UCNPs through an amidation reaction. The successful fabrication of Apt-UCNPs was proved by the strong absorption of CA125 aptamer around 260 nm (Fig. S1).

CDs were prepared from o-phenylenediamine and dopamine by a one-step hydrothermal method. The chemical composition of the CDs was studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As depicted in Fig. 2 a, three dominant peaks at 284.83, 399.9, and 532.9 eV attributing to the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively, were observed, which indicates that CDs are composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. The morphology of CDs was also characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2b, c, CDs possess very small sizes with diameter of 2.7 nm ± 1.2 nm. CDs show strong absorption around 554 nm with an extinction coefficient of 3.5 × 103 g−1·L·cm−1 (Fig. S2). The absorption band overlaps with the green emission (4S3/2 → 4I15/2) of Er3+-doped UCNPs (Fig. 2d), which ensures the effective energy transfer from UCNPs to CDs.

Apt-UCNPs can effectively assembled with CDs through π-π stacking interaction between CDs and the aptamer. The UCL intensity of Apt-UCNPs decreased with time after addition of CDs and reached equilibrium within 80 min (Fig. S3). With adding increasing amounts of CDs, the quenching efficiency gradually increases and reaches a plateau of 90% with 0.1 mg·mL−1 CDs (Fig. 3 a, b). Such a great quenching efficiency confers the nanoprobe high signal-to-background ratio (SBR) and sensitivity.

The time course of UCL recovery was firstly studied to find out the optimised incubation time for CA125 sensing. As shown in Fig. S4, the UCL intensity of the nanoprobe enhanced gradually after addition of CA125 and became stable after 100 min. Thus, the incubation time was chosen as 100 min in the following experiments to make sure the complete reaction. There is a linear relationship between the relative UCL intensity (F-F0)/F0, where F and F0 represent the UCL intensity of the nanoprobe in presence and absence of CA125, respectively, and the logarithm of CA125 concentration in the range of 0.01–100 U·mL−1 (Fig. 4 a, b). The detection limit is calculated as 9.0 × 10−3 U·mL−1, which was calculated as the concentration corresponding to 3 times of standard deviation of blank (n = 11). To exclude the potential interference originated form UCL intensity change of the nanoprobe during incubation, the photostability of UCNPs-CDs was then studied. Little UCL change was observed within 100 min (Fig. S5), which ensures the accuracy of CA125 detection. The nanoprobe also show great selectivity. As shown in Fig. S6, other proteins or amino acids cause no obvious UCL enhancement after incubation with nanoprobes.

With the great sensitivity and selectivity of the nanoprobe in aqueous buffer, we applied it for CA125 assay in complicated biological environment. CA125 assay was then conducted in 100-fold diluted human serum. As shown in Fig. 5 , UCL intensity enhances gradually with adding increasing amount of CA125. The linear relationship, in the range of 0.01–100 U·mL−1, was observed between the relative UCL intensity and the logarithm of CA125 concentration and the detection limit was 9.0 × 10−3 U·mL−1, which is similar with the detection results in aqueous buffer. These results demonstrate that the nanoprobe UCNPs-CDs is also available for CA125 detection in human serum.

In summary, we have successfully developed a CA125 biosensor UCNPs-CDs based on LRET strategy, in which UCNPs and CDs were employed as energy donor and energy acceptor, respectively. CDs were assemble with Apt-UCNPs through π-π stacking interaction, which triggered LRET process and induced the quenching of UCL. The presence of CA125 blocked the π-π stacking interaction and inhibited LRET process. The UCL intensity increased linearly with the logarithm of CA125 concentration in the range 0.01 to 100 U·mL−1. This probe with great sensitivity, selectivity and simplicity, was also available for CA125 quantification in human serum. If a smartphone was used to read out and automatically analyse the UCL signals [32], this probe could be further applied in constructing point-of-care testing (POCT) devices [33], which is promising for clinical application in early diagnosis of ovarian cancer.
Miss Xiaojing Zhang does the experiments and prepares the manuscript.
Dr. Yanying Wang helps to draw some Schematics and analysis the data.
Dr. Hongping Deng collects and treats the clinical serum samples, and helps to assay them.
Dr. Xiaoxing Xiong helps to assay the clinical serum samples.
Dr. Huijuan Zhang helps to analysis experimental data.
Dr. Tao Liang helps to design the experiments and to revise the manuscript.
Dr. Chunya Li helps to design the experiments and to revise the manuscript thoroughly.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from The National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (Nos. 21675175, 21874157 and 21275166), Major Projects of Technical Innovation of Hubei Province, China (No. 2017ACA172) and The Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province, China (Nos. 2018CFB617 and 2015CFA092).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105761.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Harnessing Wnt signaling as a targetable therapy in epithelial ovarian cancer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.A. Wall, R.C. Arend, Harnessing Wnt signaling as a targetable therapy in epithelial ovarian cancer, Ann. Transl. Med. 8(13) (2020) 837.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The ovarian cancer tumor immune microenvironment (TIME) as target for therapy: a focus on innate immunity cells as therapeutic effectors

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reactivity of a monoclonal antibody with human ovarian carcinoma.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.C. Bast, Jr., M. Feeney, H. Lazarus, L.M. Nadler, R.B. Colvin, R.C. Knapp, Reactivity of a monoclonal antibody with human ovarian carcinoma, J. Clin. Investig. 68 (1981) 1331-1337.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of the ovarian cancer biomarker CA-125 using chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer to graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Al-Ogaidi, H. Gou, Z.P. Aguilar, S. Guo, A.K. Melconian, A.K.A. Al-kazaz, F. Meng, N. Wu, Detection of the ovarian cancer biomarker CA-125 using chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer to graphene quantum dots, Chem. Commun. 50 (2014) 1344-1346.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luteal blood flow and luteal function

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Takasaki, H. Tamura, K. Taniguchi, H. Asada, T. Taketani, A. Matsuoka, Y. Yamagata, K. Shimamura, H. Morioka, N. Sugino, Luteal blood flow and luteal function, J. Ovarian Res. 2 (2009) 1.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bead-based ELISA for validation of ovarian cancer early detection markers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Scholler, M. Crawford, A. Sato, C.W. Drescher, K.C. Briant, N. Kiviat, G.L. Anderson, N. Urban, Bead-based ELISA for validation of ovarian cancer early detection markers, Clin. Cancer Res. 12 (2006) 2117.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive immunoassay for CA125 detection using acid site compound as signal and enhancer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ren, H. Wang, D. Wu, D. Fan, Y. Zhang, B. Du, Q. Wei, Ultrasensitive immunoassay for CA125 detection using acid site compound as signal and enhancer, Talanta 144 (2015) 535-541.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of tumor marker CA125 by capillary electrophoretic enzyme immunoassay with electrochemical detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. He, N. Gao, W. Jin, Determination of tumor marker CA125 by capillary electrophoretic enzyme immunoassay with electrochemical detection, Anal. Chim. Acta 497 (2003) 75-81.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Disposable electrochemical immunosensor for simultaneous assay of a panel of breast cancer tumor markers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ge, F. Yu, L. Ge, M. Yan, J. Yu, D. Chen, Disposable electrochemical immunosensor for simultaneous assay of a panel of breast cancer tumor markers, Analyst 137 (2012) 4727-4733.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microfluidic chip electrophoresis for simultaneous fluorometric aptasensing of alpha-fetoprotein, carbohydrate antigen 125 and carcinoembryonic antigen by applying a catalytic hairpin assembly

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Xie, Y. Cao, F. Hu, T. Li, Q. Wang, N. Gan, Microfluidic chip electrophoresis for simultaneous fluorometric aptasensing of alpha-fetoprotein, carbohydrate antigen 125 and carcinoembryonic antigen by applying acatalytic hairpin assembly, Microchim. Acta 186 (2019) 547.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of Tb-MOF-on-Fe-MOF conjugate as a novel platform for ultrasensitive detection of carbohydrate antigen 125 and living cancer cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Wang, M. Hu, Z. Li, L. He, Y. Song, Q. Jia, Z. Zhang, M. Du, Construction of Tb-MOF-on-Fe-MOF conjugate as a novel platform for ultrasensitive detection of carbohydrate antigen 125 and living cancer cells, Biosens. Bioelectron. 142 (2019) 111536.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Rationally Designed Upconversion Nanoprobe for in Vivo Detection of Hydroxyl Radical

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Li, T. Liang, S. Lv, Q. Zhuang, Z. Liu, A rationally designed upconversion nanoprobe for in vivo detection of hydroxyl radical, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 137 (2015) 11179-11185.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescence, electrochemiluminescence, and photoelectrochemical chemo/biosensing based on semiconductor quantum dots

Bioelectron. 96 (2017) 308-316.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aptamer and 5-fluorouracil dual-loading Ag 2 S quantum dots used as a sensitive label-free probe for near-infrared photoluminescence turn-on detection of CA125 antigen

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Respective and simultaneous detection tumor markers CA125 and STIP1 using aptamer-based fluorescent and RLS sensors
